ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kodi Vepudu; closest_parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific entry for Kodi Vepudu; A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the nearest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kodi Vepudu or a sufficiently close Andhra-style spiced chicken fry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kodi Vepudu is approximately 250 g, consisting of bone-in or boneless chicken pieces coated in a thick, dry spiced masala.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kodi Vepudu is a dry-fried Andhra chicken preparation. No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for Kodi Vepudu specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Chicken curry, cooked' (per 100 g edible portion) as the closest approved primary source for Indian chicken dishes, with adjustment context noted. USDA FoodData Central 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed' (FDC ID 171477) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat ranges. The dry-fry preparation of Kodi Vepudu results in slightly higher fat content than a standard curry due to oil absorption; values reflect a conservative mid-range estimate consistent with both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for spiced chicken preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kodi Vepudu in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate spiced chicken dishes. Chicken-based dishes with minimal starchy carbohydrate content consistently fall in the GI 25–40 range in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021). The low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) and absence of high-GI starch components support a GI of 30 (low category, ≤55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per meal, as a full restaurant serving of Kodi Vepudu can contribute approximately 1050 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — particularly when consumed alongside other sodium-containing dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low net carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kodi Vepudu has a low glycemic index of approximately 30 and a low glycemic load per serving (~3.4), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions and without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from oil-based dry-fry preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat and sodium content in Kodi Vepudu; choosing lean chicken breast, reducing oil quantity, and limiting portion to 150 g can help keep saturated fat intake within the AHA recommended limit of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kodi Vepudu is a traditional Andhra Pradesh dry-fried chicken dish from South India, widely popular in Telugu cuisine. The dish consists of chicken pieces — typically bone-in or boneless — marinated in a blend of red chilli powder, turmeric, ginger-garlic paste, cumin, coriander, and garam masala, then stir-fried or dry-fried in oil with onions, green chillies, and curry leaves until the masala coats the chicken in a thick, aromatic crust. The result is a deeply spiced, slightly crispy exterior with tender, juicy meat inside. The flavour profile is bold, pungent, and intensely savoury with prominent heat from both red and green chillies. Kodi Vepudu is commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice, roti, or as part of a larger South Indian meal spread. In Singapore, it is served at South Indian restaurants as a main protein dish. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. The sodium content is elevated due to spice blends and added salt, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is Kodi Vepudu good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Kodi Vepudu as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken values. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for low-carbohydrate chicken dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct Kodi Vepudu entry.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked chicken data and are marked as adapted. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for Kodi Vepudu exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is served at Manam - Upper Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 24.00. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (South Indian restaurant in Singapore) but is not independently verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 100 = (30 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.375, rounded to 3.4.
